abstract:
Invasive fungal infections (IFIs) have emerged as a significant global health threat, driven largely by an expanding population of immunocompromised patients. In these high-risk clinical scenarios, early and precise diagnosis is the linchpin of effective management, as it allows for aggressive containment before infections progress to life-threatening stages. While traditional microscopy and culture techniques remain foundational to clinical mycology, they are increasingly supplemented or replaced by a new generation of sophisticated diagnostic tools. 

This review provides a comprehensive and orderly update on the rapidly evolving landscape of fungal diagnostics. We explore the transition from conventional methods to advanced non-culture-based technologies, including novel PCR assays, T2 Candida magnetic resonance, microfluidic chips, and next-generation sequencing. Furthermore, we delve into pioneering frontiers such as nanotechnology-based biosensors and artificial intelligence-driven predictive models. To our knowledge, this is the first review to integrate these diverse fields—particularly the intersection of biosensor technology and machine learning—within the specific context of IFI. By bridging the gap between traditional algorithms and these emerging adjuncts, this work serves as a vital resource for the scientific and medical communities to enhance diagnostic accuracy and improve patient outcomes.
